coredata.dc:title: Platelet-inspired nanomedicine targeting activated neutrophils to alleviate ulcerative colitis by free radicals scavenging and controlled neutrophil swarming
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Xiangji
Song, Jian
Zhang, Yujie
Yang, Mei
Deng, Zhichao
Gao, Bowen
Zhu, Yuanyuan
Xu, Chenxi
Ding, Chenguang
Zhang, Mingzhen
Zhang, Baojun
coredata.dc:description: Ulcerative colitis (UC) is a chronic non-specific inflammatory disease that causes inflammation and ulcers in the intestinal tract. Current therapies focus on the relief of symptoms in the short-term while it is an urgent need to achieve long-term remission of inflammation in clinics. In the current study, Platinum-doped carbon nanodot (CD) nanozymes (PtCD) with cascade enzymatic activities for superoxide dismutase/catalase (SOD/CAT) were successfully synthesized. PtCD and spleen tyrosine kinase (SYK) inhibitor piceatannol were co-loaded in PLGA and coated with platelet membrane to form a platelet-inspired nanomedicine (PM@Pic/PtCD@NP). PtCD-loaded nanoparticles (PtCD@NP) were found to co-locate with mitochondria, scavenge reactive oxygen species (ROS), and reduce the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines. In addition, the biomimetic modification with the platelet membrane enabled PM@Pic/PtCD@NP to hitchhike neutrophils to the site of inflammation and release piceatannol to inhibit neutrophil swarming. Intravenous administration of PM@Pic/PtCD@NP partially reversed and prevented intestinal inflammation and intestinal barrier dysfunction in a murine model of colitis. The RNA-sequencing analysis revealed that gene markers of mitochondrial respiratory chain complex Ⅰ were significantly associated with PM@Pic/PtCD@NP treatment. Collectively, This study suggests that PM@Pic/PtCD@NP treatment may serve as an innovative therapeutic strategy for the long-term remission of UC.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of platelet-inspired nanomedicine platform targeting activated neutrophils to alleviate UC by cascade SOD/CAT nanozymes-mediated free radicals scavenging and controlled neutrophils swarming. (A) Preparation of platelet-inspired nanomedicine platform PM@Pic/PtCD@NP. (B) The biomimetic modification of the platelet membrane enabled it to hitchhike to the site of inflammation by neutrophils, where the released Pic promotes the detachment of neutrophils from endothelial cells into the circulation, resulting in decreased neutrophil infiltration. The other part of the NPs entering the lamina propria could release PtCD that scavenged ROS, reduce the expression of pro-inflammatory factors, and restore the function of the intestinal barrier.

Characterization of PtCD, PtCD@NP, and PM@PtCD@NP. (A) TEM images of PtCD. The image on the right showed the zoom area in the white box. (B) Particle size distribution of PtCD. (C) Zeta potential of PtCD. (D) Element mapping for PtCD. (E) TEM images of PtCD@NP. (F) TEM images of PM@PtCD@NP. The white line represents the coating of the platelet membrane. (G) Zeta-potential of platelet membrane (PM), PtCD@NP, and PM@PtCD@NP. (H) Particle size distribution of PtCD@NP and PM@PtCD@NP. (I) Cumulative drug release curve. (J) Western blotting of platelet, PM, and PM@PtCD@NP (n = 3). (K) SDS-PAGE protein analysis of platelet (I), PM (II), and PM@PtCD@NP (III).

The cascade free radicals scavenging activities of PtCD and PtCD@NP nanozymes. (A) Schematic presentation of cascade free radicals scavenging activities of PtCD nanozymes. (B) The ability of PtCD and PtCD@NP to remove superoxide anions was determined by the NBT method (n = 4). (C) The SOD-like activity of PtCD and PtCD@NP was measured by a commercial SOD assay kit (n = 3). (D) The CAT-like activity of PtCD, PtCD@NP, and Blank@NP was measured by monitoring the dissolved O2 at the same concentrations of 33.34 μg/mL. (E) The H2O2 scavenging of PtCD, PtCD@NP, and Blank@NP with the concentration of 25 μg/mL by recording the absorbance of 240 nm in a time-driven mode. (F) Typical kinetic curves of oxygen generation from the decomposition of H2O2 catalyzed by PtCD and PtCD@NP (n = 3). (G) The mixed solution Fenton reagent and MB in the absence and presence of PtCD, PtCD@NP, and Blank@NP with the same concentration of 25 μg/mL. (H) The·OH scavenging ability of NPs was detected by the TA method, and the spectral change of the solution was detected by fluorescence spectrometer. (I) The total anti-oxidation capacity of the PtCD and PtCD@NP of different concentrations (n = 4).

ROS-scavenging and anti-inflammatory ability of PtCD and PtCD@NP nanozymes. (A) Fluorescence images of the co-localization between PtCD@NP and mitochondria. (B) The profile analysis of (A). (C) Fluorescence images of the co-localization between PtCD@NP and lysosomes. (D) The profile analysis of (C). (E) Intracellular SOD-like activity. (F) Flow cytometry results of (E). (G) Intracellular CAT-like activity. (H) Flow cytometry results of (H). (I) Intracellular ROS-scavenging capability. (J) Flow cytometry results of (E). (K) The mRNA expression of different inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, IL-12, and IL-10) in different groups. (n = 3, *** P < 0.001, compared with the LPS group.).

Evaluation of the targeting ability of PM@PtCD@NP both in vivo and in vitro. (A) DiL-labeled PtCD@NP and PM@PtCD@NP were efficiently internalized by neutrophils. Here, NPs were labeled by DiL (red), and the nucleus was stained by DAPI (blue). The white box represents the zoom area. (B) The cellular uptake efficiency of NP by neutrophils was quantified by flow cytometry. (C) Fluorescence images of DSS-induced colitis mice, organs, and colon tissues at indicated time points after intravenous injection of PM@PtCD@NP. H: heart; Li: liver; S: spleen; Lu: lung; K: kidney. (D) Quantification of the fluorescence intensity in vivo (n = 3). (E) Quantification of the fluorescence intensity of major organs (n = 3). (F) Quantification of the fluorescence intensity of colon tissues (n = 3). * * P < 0.01, * ** P < 0.001.

Biocompatibility assessment of PM@Pic/PtCD@NP. (A) Relative viability of RAW 264.7 cells treated with PM@Pic/PtCD@NP for 24 h (n = 6). (B) Relative viability of RAW 264.7 cells treated with PM@Pic/PtCD@NP for 48 h (n = 6). (C) Weight change curves of mice treated with different doses of Pic/PtCD@NP within 7 days (n = 4). (D) Hemolysis activity of PM@Pic/PtCD@NP (n = 3). (E) Flow cytometry analysis of apoptosis of Colon-26 and RAW 264.7 cells treated with Pic/PtCD@NP for 24 h. (F) H&E staining of major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) isolated from the mice receiving Pic/PtCD@NP via intravenous administration. The blue box indicates the enlarged area. Scale bar (Spleen): 300 µm. Scale bar (Zoom): 100 µm. Scale bar of the others: 700 µm. (G) The blood routine assays (n = 4). (H) The biochemical assays of the serum were collected from the mice receiving PM@Pic/PtCD@NP via intravenous administration (n = 4).

Evaluation of the therapeutic effect of PM@Pic/PtCD@NP in murine colitis model. (A) Schematic illustration of the establishment and treatment of colitis mice. (B) Weight change curves of mice within 7 days (n = 6; ** P < 0.01 compared with the control group; the X-axis represents days). (C) DAI curves within 7 days (n = 6; ** P < 0.01 compared with the control group; the X-axis represents days). (D) The length of the colon (n = 6). (E) Images of the colons. (F) Endoscopic images of mice. (G) The mRNA expression of different inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-12, IL-6, IL-1β, and IL-10). I represents DSS + PM@Pic@NP group, II represents DSS + PM@PtCD@NP group, Ⅲ represents DSS + PM@Pic/PtCD@NP group (n = 6). (H) Immunofluorescence image of tight junction protein (occludin, ZO-1, and claudin-1). ## P < 0.01, ### P < 0.001, the DSS group compared with the control group. * * P < 0.01, * ** P < 0.001, NPs-treated group compared with the DSS group. Ns, no significance.

Evaluation of “neutrophil braking” ability of the PM@Pic/PtCD@NP. (A) Flow cytometry of the proportion of neutrophils in the lamina propria of the colon. (B) H&E staining of colonic sections. The blue box indicates the zoom area. The scale of the image above is 200 µm, and that of the bottom is 60 µm. (C) Immunofluorescence images of MPO in the colons.

RNA-sequence analysis of the colons from colitis mice receiving PM@Pic/PtCD@NP treatment. (A) Venn diagram. (B) The volcano plots identified the upregulated and downregulated genes after PM@Pic/PtCD@NP treatment. (C) Heat maps for differentially expressed genes in the control group (Ⅰ), DSS group (Ⅱ), and DSS + PM@Pic/PtCD@NP group (Ⅲ). (D) GO enrichment analysis of downregulated genes after PM@Pic/PtCD@NP treatment compared to the DSS group. (E) KEGG pathway enrichment analysis of the identified differentially-expressed genes. (F-G) GSEA showed the enrichment of the GO process in the DSS group and PM@Pic/PtCD@NP group.

Evaluation of the preventive effect of PM@Pic/PtCD@NP in murine colitis model. (A) Schematic illustration of the establishment and treatment of colitis mice. (B) Weight change curves of mice within 7 days (n = 6; ** P < 0.01 compared with the control group; the X-axis represents days). (C) DAI curves within 7 days (n = 6; ** P < 0.01 compared with the control group; the X-axis represents days). (D) The length of the colon (n = 6). (E) MPO activity (n = 3). (F) Endoscopic images of mice. (G) H&E staining results. (H) Immunofluorescence image of tight junction protein (occludin, ZO-1, and claudin-1). (I) The mRNA expression of different inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-12, IL-6, IL-1β, and IL-10). I represents DSS + PM@Pic@NP group, II represents DSS + PM@PtCD@NP group, Ⅲ represents DSS + PM@Pic/PtCD@NP group (n = 6). # P < 0.05, ### P < 0.001, the DSS group compared with the control group. * * P < 0.01, * ** P < 0.001, NPs-treated group compared with the DSS group. Ns, no significance.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ulcerative colitis (UC) is a chronic non-specific inflammatory disease that causes inflammation and ulcers in the intestinal tract. Current therapies focus on the relief of symptoms in the short-term while it is an urgent need to achieve long-term remission of inflammation in clinics. In the current study, Platinum-doped carbon nanodot (CD) nanozymes (PtCD) with cascade enzymatic activities for superoxide dismutase/catalase (SOD/CAT) were successfully synthesized. PtCD and spleen tyrosine kinase (SYK) inhibitor piceatannol were co-loaded in PLGA and coated with platelet membrane to form a platelet-inspired nanomedicine (PM@Pic/PtCD@NP). PtCD-loaded nanoparticles (PtCD@NP) were found to co-locate with mitochondria, scavenge reactive oxygen species (ROS), and reduce the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines. In addition, the biomimetic modification with the platelet membrane enabled PM@Pic/PtCD@NP to hitchhike neutrophils to the site of inflammation and release piceatannol to inhibit neutrophil swarming. Intravenous administration of PM@Pic/PtCD@NP partially reversed and prevented intestinal inflammation and intestinal barrier dysfunction in a murine model of colitis. The RNA-sequencing analysis revealed that gene markers of mitochondrial respiratory chain complex Ⅰ were significantly associated with PM@Pic/PtCD@NP treatment. Collectively, This study suggests that PM@Pic/PtCD@NP treatment may serve as an innovative therapeutic strategy for the long-term remission of UC.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ulcerative colitis (UC) is a chronic inflammatory disease that often occurs in the colon, and is known as the inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) along with Crohn’s disease (CD) [1,2]. In recent decades, the incidence of UC has increased worldwide, especially in developing countries.[3] Genetic susceptibility, intestinal immune disorders, oxidative imbalance, and epithelial barrier abnormalities are the main pathogenesis of UC [4]. Mucosal inflammation in patients with UC starts from the rectum and extends to the proximal colon. The common clinical manifestations are abdominal pain, diarrhea, and bloody stool [5,6].

During the occurrence and development of UC, a large number of neutrophils infiltrate the intestinal mucosa lamina propria and then produce excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) [7–9]. ROS are single-electron reduction products of oxygen in vivo, including superoxide anion (O2 •-), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), hydroxyl radical (·OH), etc. In addition to directly causing damage to the intestinal mucosa, ROS also induces oxidative stress and a variety of lipid peroxidation in epithelial cells of the colon, which further damages the colon epithelium [10]. ROS also have the chemotactic ability to recruit neutrophils and other immune cells to enter the inflammatory site and stimulate the secretion of pro-inflammatory factors in large quantities, thereby amplifying the intestinal inflammatory response and exacerbating damage. In addition, oxidation increases the expression level of the cell adhesion molecule family, which also recruits immune cells into the inflammation site and leads to the deterioration of the disease [11]. As the elevated level of ROS at the inflammatory site is one of the major pathogenesis of UC, [12] scavenging ROS and reducing oxidative stress reaction is one of the major treatment strategies for UC.

The natural anti-oxidant enzyme system in vivo mainly includes superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT) and glutathione peroxidase (GPx), etc. These enzymes constitute the complete anti-oxidant chain in the body and maintain ROS at normal levels under physiological conditions [13,14]. However, most natural enzymes are proteins, which are often associated with issues such as variability, easy loss of activity, cumbersome purification process, and difficulty in storage and recovery. Nanozymes are a type of nano-materials with enzymatic properties. Compared with natural enzymes, nanozymes have better physicochemical stability, versatility, and recyclability [15–18]. We previously developed carbon dot (CD) nanozymes with SOD-like activity. However, the final catalytic product of CD is H2O2 and high concentrations of H2O2 still trigger oxidative damage and cause cell damage [19]. In the current study, CD was combined with platinum (Pt), which has CAT-like activity and can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into H2O and O2. Platinum/carbon dot nanozymes (PtCD) with SOD/CAT cascade enzymatic activity might achieve better anti-oxidant effects in UC.

Moreover, neutrophils are important intestinal immune cells. When the body is invaded by bacteria or pathogens, neutrophils migrate to the injured area and release inflammatory mediators to recruit more neutrophils or other defense cells. Persistent activation and over-recruitment of neutrophils are characteristic signs of UC [20]. When neutrophils are excessively activated, normal tissues are damaged [21,22]. Therefore, therapeutic strategies targeting neutrophils to control the exacerbation of inflammation include inhibition of inflammation driven by neutrophil extracellular traps, interference with the aggregation of neutrophils at the inflammatory site, promotion of neutrophils “reverse migration” from the tissues into the bloodstream, acceleration of the consumption of neutrophils at the inflammatory site, and enhancement of the apoptosis of neutrophils [23,24]. For example, spleen tyrosine kinase (SYK) inhibitor piceatannol (Pic), through blocking the integrin signal transduction in neutrophils and reducing the activity of over-activated neutrophil adhesion molecules, inhibits neutrophil adhesion and aggregation, reduces inflammatory reaction, and restores the “braking” of neutrophils [25,26].

Therefore, this work utilized poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA)-based polymer that encapsulated both hydrophilic PtCD nanozymes and hydrophobic natural small molecule compound Pic. On the one hand, it improved the long circulation time of small-sized PtCD nanozymes in vivo, prolonged the half-life in blood, and improved the therapeutic effect. On the other hand, it improved the solubility and bioavailability of Pic in aqueous phase. PtCD cascades nanozymes with SOD- and CAT-like activity was firstly synthesized and further prepared by the PLGA-based polymer co-loaded with PtCD cascade nanozymes and Pic. The biomimetic modification of the platelet membrane enabled it to hitchhike to the site of inflammation by neutrophils, where the released Pic promoted the detachment of neutrophils from endothelial cells into the circulation, resulting in decreased neutrophil infiltration. The other part of the nanoparticles (NPs) entering the lamina propria could release PtCD, thus scavenging ROS, reducing the expression of pro-inflammatory factors, and restoring the function of the intestinal barrier ( Fig. 1). The therapeutic and prevention effects of the combination of PtCD and Pic on UC were evaluated in the murine colitis models, and the role of free radicals scavenging and controlled neutrophil swarming was also clarified.

Lactide: glycolide (50: 50) (PLGA, wt: 38,000–54,000), poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA, 86%−89% hydrolyzed, low molecular weight). Pic was acquired from Meilun Bio (Dalian, China). Fluorescent dyes (DiL and DiR) were obtained and purchased from Promokine (Heidelberg, Germany). Superoxide dismutase assay kit (S311) was purchased from Dojindo Molecular Technologies Co., Ltd. Mito-Tracker Green, total superoxide dismutase assay kit with WST-8, 2′, 7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), and dihydroethidium (DHE) were purchased from Beyotime Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. APC/Cy7-CD45, FITC-CD11b, and PE-Ly6G were purchased from Proteintech.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PtCD, PtCD@NP, Pic/PtCD@NP, and PM@Pic/PtCD@NP
ce:section.ce:para: Each bottle was processed sequentially by 1) the addition of 1.6 mL carbon dot with activated carbon (1 mg/mL) as a raw material; 2) the addition of 50 μL chloroplatinic acid (H₂PtCl₆·6 H2O) (40 mM) and is mixed at room temperature; 3) Addition of 270 μL ultra-pure water and being stirred at room temperature for 10 min; 4) Addition of 80 μL NaOH solution with a concentration of 1 M and being stirred at room temperature for 10 min；5) Addition of 3 mL NaBH4 solution (10 mg/mL) and is stirred at room temperature for 3 h with a rotating speed of 350 rpm; 6) The solvent was evaporated with a rotary evaporator, and the remaining 2 mL PtCD solution was reserved or freeze-dried for storage.

PtCD@NP and Pic/PtCD@NP were synthesized by the microemulsion method. For the preparation of PtCD@NP, 50 mg PLGA was dissolved in 2 mL dichloromethane (DCM). For the preparation of Pic/PtCD@NP, 50 mg PLGA and Pic were dissolved in 2 mL DCM. 300 μL PtCD solution was added to 2 mL DCM drop by drop, and ultrasound was performed in an ice bath for 2 min with 25% power. After ultrasound, the emulsion was added drop by drop into 4 mL 2.5% PVA solution, and ultrasound was performed again in an ice bath for 2 min with 25% power. Added 36 mL of ultra-pure water and 4 mL of PVA solution, then added 50 mL of ultra-pure water. DCM was removed by a rotary evaporator. Stirred at room temperature, 300 rpm, 4 h. Centrifugation at the speed of 13000 rpm, 15 min. After washing twice, the precipitation was suspended with 300 μL ultra-pure water. Added an equal volume of cryoprotectant, stored at − 80 ℃, and freeze-dried for storage.

1–10 mL of mouse plasma was collected and centrifuged at the speed of 220 g for 10 min. The upper serum was collected and platelets were extracted with a platelet extraction kit. The extracted platelets were stored at − 80 ℃, thawed at room temperature, and then placed at − 80 ℃, and the freezing and thawing steps were repeated more than three times to obtain platelet membranes. The obtained platelet membrane (1 mg/mL) was mixed with NPs in the same volume, and ultrasonic treatment was carried out at the power of 40 kHz 80 w 5 min and 40 kHz 100 w 5 min. The platelet membrane was successfully coated on the surface of NPs.

The particle size and zeta potential of NPs were measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) method. The NPs were dissolved in 1 mL of ultra-pure water and measured by a Malvern analyzer 3 times for each sample.

The NPs were dissolved in ultra-pure water, 10 μL was dropped on the copper net for transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and waited for 5 min at room temperature. After drying, the copper mesh was used for TEM observation and dyed with uranyl acetate in advance.

The coating of the platelet membrane was characterized by the Coomassie brilliant blue method. A total of 1 g of coomassie brilliant blue R250 was dissolved in ultra-pure water, and 100 mL of glacial acetic acid and 250 mL of isopropyl alcohol were added, with a constant volume of 1000 mL. The volume of protein and loading buffer were calculated according to the protein concentration. After sample addition, electrophoresis was carried out, the excess gel was removed after the end, and the remaining gel was transferred to Coomassie brilliant blue solution, and incubated for 1 h on a horizontal shaking table. The gel was decolorized by a decolorizing solution prepared with 100 mL glacial acetic acid, 50 mL ethanol, and 850 mL ultra-pure water, and washed until the gel was decolorized, and the protein bands were clearly visible.

Western blotting was also used to characterize the coating of platelet membrane, which was divided into platelet group, platelet membrane group, and platelet membrane coated NPs group, and 12% SDS-PAGE was used for Western blotting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The SOD- and CAT-like activity of PtCD and PtCD@NP
ce:section.ce:para: The SOD-like activity was verified by the NBT method. NBT solution with a concentration of 0.05 mM, L-met solution with a concentration of 13 mM, and riboflavin with a concentration of 20 μM was dissolved in PBS buffer solution with a concentration of 25 mM and pH value of 7.4. Different concentrations of NPs were mixed with the above solution, and irradiated with a light emitting diode (LED) lamp for 5 min, and the absorbance at 560 nm was measured with a microplate reader. The SOD-like activity was also determined with the total superoxide dismutase assay kit (S311–10, Dojindo Molecular Technologies), and the experiment was carried out according to the instructions.

The CAT-like activity was detected by the dissolved oxygen analyzer. The concentration of NPs was 500 μg/mL and the volume was 15 mL. After calibrating the dissolved oxygen meter, 180 μL H2O2 solution with 30% concentration was added and recorded by the probe. The K
M constant was obtained by calculating the dissolved oxygen in a 500 μg/mL NPs solution containing 30% H2O2 in different volumes (10, 20, 40, 80, 100, 160 μL). The·OH scavenging ability of NPs was verified by the terephthalic acid (TA) method and methylene blue (MB) method.

After adhesion, RAW 264.7 cells were incubated in a medium containing NPs (200 μg/mL) for 8 h. The medium was discarded and cells were washed twice by PBS before the addition of ROSup and incubation for 0.5 h. Then superoxide anion probe DHE, oxygen-specific quenching fluorescence probe [Ru(dpp)3]Cl2 (RDPP), and active oxygen probe DCFH-DA was added. After the experiment, the glass slide was used for microscope observation, and the remaining cells were collected for flow cytometry.

After RAW 264.7 cells were co-incubated with NPs for 4 h, Mito-Tracker/Lyso-Tracker was added into the cell medium according to different proportions and continued to incubate at 37 ℃ for 45 min. The medium was removed and cells were washed twice with PBS before taking out the glass climbing plate and sealing it with DAPI.

Neutrophils from mice were obtained using a neutrophil extraction kit (Tianjin, Haoyang Biological Manufacture Co., Ltd). If the extract contained a small amount of red blood cells, red blood cell lysate was added for cleavage. The obtained neutrophils were stained with Wright-Giemsa dye.

After NPs and RAW 264.7 cells were incubated, the cells were induced to inflammation by lipopolysaccharide (LPS). Cells were collected for RNA extraction and reverse transcription. Then RT-qPCR experiment was used to study the mRNA expression of inflammatory factors.

The extracted neutrophils were cultured with RPMI1640 medium at 37 ℃ and 200 μg/mL NPs were added for 2 h. Phorbol myristate acetate (PMA) was used to mimic the inflammatory site. The cells were collected and 4% paraformaldehyde was added for 20 min. The paraformaldehyde was removed by centrifugation and washed twice with PBS, and then the cells were re-suspended with 50 μL PBS and then dripped onto the slide. The cells on the slide were observed with a fluorescence microscope following the addition of a drop of DAPI and slide-sealing.

DiR-labeled PSB@NP-FA was prepared for an in vivo targeting experiment. After intravenous administration, an in vivo imaging system (IVIS) was used to trace NPs in vivo and obtain fluorescence images at different time points. The mice were then sacrificed and the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) and colon were collected for imaging. The fluorescence intensity of the major organs and colon was reflected by the histogram.

The obtained colons were fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde, which was changed into 30% sucrose solution before sectioning. The tissues were wrapped with OCT and sliced by cryotome. The cytoskeleton was stained with phalloidin-FITC, the plates were sealed with DAPI, and the localization of DiL-labeled NPs in the colon was observed by microscope.

Mice were injected intravenously with DiR-labeled NPs. Mice were sacrificed at different time points after injection, was obtained serum, and the fluorescence intensity of DiR in serum was detected by fluorescence spectrophotometer. The half-life of NPs in blood was calculated by a two-phase decay model by GraphPad Prism 8.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo and in vitro safety evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: RAW 264.7 cells and Colon-26 cells were cultured in a medium with different concentrations of NPs for 24 h after adhesion. After the experiment, cells were collected and re-suspended with 100 μL 1 ×binding buffer. Annexin V-FITC (5 μL) and PI (10 μL) solution were added to each sample, mixed, and reacted at room temperature without light for 10–15 min 400 μL 1 ×binding buffer was added to each tube and mixed before the detection by flow cytometry. In addition, the biosafety of NPs at the cellular level was also detected by the methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) method.

To test the biocompatibility of NPs in vivo, mice were injected intravenously with saline or NPs every other day, the weight changes were recorded every day. On day 8, the mice were sacrificed, and blood was collected for routine blood analysis and biochemical analysis. Major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) and colons were obtained and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde, and then hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining was used to observe the histological changes.

To verify the preventive effect of NPs on colitis, female C57BL/6 (6–8 weeks) mice with a body weight of about 20 g were selected, which were purchased from the Medical Experimental Animal Center of Xi’an Jiaotong University, Shaanxi Province, China. There were at least 6 mice in each group, randomly assigned. After adaptation for 1 week, mice were given 2.5% dextran sulfate sodium (DSS) for 7 days to induce acute colitis. The NPs was administered intravenously on day 1, 3, 5, and 7. Body weight, fecal condition, and disease activity index (DAI) were observed and recorded daily. The mice were sacrificed on the last day of the experiment, and the distal colon was taken for subsequent experimental analysis.

To verify the therapeutic effect of NPs on colitis, mice were given 2.5% DSS for 5 days and stopped on day 6. The NPs was administered intravenously on day 6, 7, 8, and 9. Other treatments are the same as the above. All experiments complied with the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee at Xi’an Jiaotong University.

The colon sections were put into antigen repair solution (Tris-EDTA solution with pH 9), heated and boiled for 10 min, and then cooled for 35 min. Samples were washed with PBS 3 times (5 min each time) and were incubated with blocking buffer at room temperature for 1 h. The myeloperoxidase (MPO) primary antibody was incubated at room temperature for 2 h or at 4 ℃ overnight. Following the wash with PBS (3 times, 5 min each time), the second antibody was added, incubated in the dark for 1 h at room temperature, and washed with PBS 3 times for 5 min each time. Samples were sealed with DAPI and observed with a microscope.

The sections were fixed with − 20 ℃ pre-cooled ethanol at room temperature for 10 min and washed with PBS 3 times, 5 min each time. 0.2% Triton X-100 diluted with PBS was incubated at room temperature for 5 min. After the wash with PBS (3 times, 3 min each time), samples were incubated with a blocking buffer at room temperature for 1 h. The blocking buffer was sucked out and incubated at room temperature for 2 h or at 4 ℃ overnight with a primary antibody of tight junction protein. After the wash with PBS (3 times, 5 min each time), the slides were sealed with DAPI and observed with a fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Neutrophil extraction from the lamina propria
ce:section.ce:para: The distal colon of mice was collected, and cut into about 2 cm length, and the colon tissue was pre-digested. The formula of the pre-digested solution: 50 mL PBS with 0.5 M ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) total 0.5 mL, 1 M dithiothreitol (DTT) total 0.05 mL, 2.5 mL FBS and 1 M HEPES total 0.75 mL. The colon was added with 6 mL of pre-digested liquid, placed in a 15 mL centrifuge tube, placed in a 37 ℃ table constant temperature oscillator shaker, and oscillated horizontally at 100 rpm for 20 min. After the oscillation and the vortex (20 s), the above steps were repeated once. The pre-digested colon tissue was transferred to a culture dish and cut into pieces as small as possible. The tissue was digested by 3 mL of digestive fluid in a 15 mL centrifuge tube at 37 ℃ for 45 min (the tube was placed in a bench 37 ℃ constant temperature oscillator shaker oscillating horizontally for 45 min at a speed of 220 rpm). The formula of the digestive fluid was as follows: 50 mL of RPMI-1640 medium with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 25 mg DNase I (deoxyribonuclease I), 150 mg Dispase II (neutral protease), 25 mg collagenase D, and 750 μL of 1 M HEPES. After being vortexed for 30 s, the mixture was transferred to a centrifuge tube with PBS at a constant volume of 35 mL and centrifuged at 1000 g at 4 ℃ for 10 min. Cell precipitation was suspended with 8 mL 40% percoll. 4 mL 80% percoll solution was added into a 15 mL centrifuge tube, slowly added 8 mL 40% percoll cell was re-suspension of the previous step onto 4 mL 80% percoll solution, and continued centrifugation for 20 min, 800 g. About 4 mL of cell suspension from the middle layer was taken into a new 15 mL centrifuge tube, and centrifuged to 14 mL with PBS at 800 g at 4 ℃ for 10 min. After the supernatant was abandoned, the cells were precipitated by 500 μL PBS. Then the cells were incubated with antibodies at 4 ℃ for 30 min without light for flow cytometry.

All the results were statistically analyzed by GraphPad Prism 8.0 software. Student-t-test was used for comparison between the two groups. The results were presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). P < 0.05 indicated a statistically significant difference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of PtCD, PM@PtCD@NP
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, platinum-doped carbon nanodot (CD) nanozymes (PtCD) were synthesized. The TEM image of PtCDs showed that the particle size was uniform and about 3 nm, which had a lattice structure, and the zeta potential was − 27.47 ± 2.36 mV ( Fig. 2A-C). The atomic force microscopy (AFM) image of PtCD demonstrated their particle height was less than 10 nm (Fig. S1). The results of the energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) showed that a large number of C, O, and Pt were irregularly doped in PtCDs, indicating that PtCDs were mainly composed of C, O, and Pt (Fig. 2D). Secondly, PLGA-based polymer encapsulated PtCDs (PtCD@NP) were synthesized by microemulsion technique to avoid the disadvantage that PtCDs were easily eliminated from the body due to their small size. Thirdly, the PtCD@NP was coated with a platelet membrane to synthesize the biomimetic nanocarrier PM@PtCD@NP, which was expected to improve the targeting property of PtCD@NP and prolong its circulation time in vivo. The TEM images showed that PtCD@NP and PM@PtCD@NP were spherical and uniform in size, and PM@PtCD@NP exhibited a clear platelet membrane on the surface (Fig. 2E and F). And the zeta potential of the platelet membrane, PtCD@NP, and PM@PtCD@NP was about − 28.90 mV, − 46.65 mV, and − 36.75 mV, respectively (Fig. 2G). The particle size of PtCD@NP was approximately 190.14 nm, while the particle size of PM@PtCD@NP was slightly larger, about 220.19 nm (Fig. 2H). Moreover, the cumulative drug release in PBS solution with pH 7.4 of PtCD@NP reached 91.98% within 48 h, while that of PM@PtCD@NP was 72.50% (Fig. 2I). Next, the expression of platelet membrane proteins CD47 and CD62P was detected by Western blotting, and the results showed that the membrane protein was successfully retained on the PM@PtCD@NP (Fig. 2J). The SDS-PAGE analyses presented that the protein profile of the NP group (Ⅲ) was consistent with those of the platelet membrane (Ⅱ) (Fig. 2K). All the results demonstrated that the biomimetic nanocarriers were successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enzymatic activity of PtCD and PtCD@NP
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized PtCD had SOD/CAT cascade enzymatic activity, in which CD exhibited SOD-like activity to catalyze O2 •- to produce H2O2 and Pt displayed CAT-like activity to catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into H2O and O2 ( Fig. 3A). In the following experiments, the content of PtCD in PtCD@NP was the same as that of PtCD. Firstly, the SOD-like activity of PtCD and PtCD@NP was investigated by the nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) reduction method. When the concentration of PtCD and PtCD in PtCD@NP was both 100 μg/mL, more than 60% of O2 •- was removed, indicating that both PtCD and PtCD@NP had high SOD-like activity (Fig. 3B). The SOD-like activity of PtCD and PtCD@NP was further quantitatively verified by a commercial WST-1 assay kit. The SOD-like activity of PtCD and PtCD@NP was calculated to be 8387.1 U/mg and 7892.91 U/mg, respectively (Fig. 3C).

Next, the CAT-like activity of PtCD and PtCD@NP was analyzed by a dissolved oxygen analyzer. The results showed that compared with the control group and Blank@NP group, the amount of dissolved oxygen was increased with time in the PtCD and PtCD@NP group, indicating that both PtCD and PtCD@NP had CAT-like activity to catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into H2O and O2, while Blank@NP had no CAT-like activity (Fig. 3D). The absorption curve of H2O2 at 240 nm was measured by a UV spectrophotometer and recorded every 2 min for 30 min. The UV absorption value of the control group and the Blank@NP group did not change significantly within 30 min (Fig. 3E). However, after the addition of PtCD or PtCD@NP, the absorption value of H2O2 at 240 nm gradually decreased with time, indicating that H2O2 was consumed (Fig. 3E). The Michaelis-Menten kinetics were calculated to be K
M = 15.79 × 10−3 M and V
max = 1.108 × 10−7 M·min−1, indicating that PtCD@NP had high affinity for substrate and CAT-like activity (Fig. 3F). In summary, PtCD@NP maintained the CAT-like activity of PtCD after PLGA encapsulation.

Hydroxyl radicals (·OH) are a type of ROS and excessive·OH causes protein denaturation and DNA damage.[27] Effectively scavenging·OH maintains cellular oxidative homeostasis. Methylene blue (MB) has a characteristic absorption peak at 664 nm and can be oxidized by·OH, which results in a decrease in its absorption peak. The results showed that the MB-only group had an obvious absorption peak at 664 nm, while the absorption disappeared in the Fenton reaction group (MB + H2O2 + Fe2+), suggesting the Fenton reaction effectively produced·OH (Fig. 3G). Furthermore, the addition of PtCD or PtCD@NP restored the absorption peak, whereas the addition of Blank@NP did not, indicating that PtCD and PtCD@NP effectively scavenged·OH, while the Blank@NP didn’t (Fig. 3G). The·OH scavenging ability of PtCD and PtCD@NP was also determined by the terephthalic acid (TA) method. The results showed only ultraviolet irradiation (UV + TA) exhibited no fluorescence characteristic peak, suggesting UV treatment didn’t produce·OH (Fig. 3H). However, the H2O2 solution with ultraviolet irradiation (UV + TA + H2O2) had an obvious peak at 425 nm, indicating a large amount of·OH was produced (Fig. 3H). In addition, after adding PtCD or PtCD@NP, the fluorescence characteristic peak decreased significantly, indicating that the·OH generated was effectively cleared by PtCD or PtCD@NP, but not Blank@NP (Fig. 3H). The above MB method and TA method verified that PtCD and PtCD@NP had·OH scavenging ability. The total antioxidant capacity of PtCD and PtCD@NP was further determined by the 2, 2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) method. Results showed that the ability of PtCD or PtCD@NP to scavenge free radicals was concentration-dependent (Fig. 3I).

Mitochondria are the main organelles in the cells that produce ROS, we tested if PtCD@NP is co-located with mitochondria to scavenge ROS after being internalized by the cells.[28,29] The fluorescence images showed that PtCD@NP labeled by DiL was co-localized with the mitochondria ( Fig. 4A). In addition, the profile analysis showed that the red peak (PtCD@NP) and green peak (Mito-Tracker) were almost parallel, and the Pearson’s correlation coefficient (PCC) was 0.91, indicating that PtCD@NP and mitochondria were well co-located (Fig. 4B). Moreover, the co-localization of PtCD@NP with lysosomes was also observed, and the fluorescence images showed that PtCD@NP was partially co-located with the lysosome, and the PCC was 0.74, indicating that some PtCD@NP entered the lysosome (Fig. 4C and D). In summary, PtCD@NP is not only co-localized with mitochondria but also partially co-localized with lysosomes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The intracellular anti-oxidant properties of PtCD@NP
ce:section.ce:para: The protective effect of PtCD and PtCD@NP on H2O2-induced oxidative damage was verified by MTT experiments. Results showed that compared with the control group, H2O2 treatment significantly reduced the cell viability of RAW 264.7 cells, down to about 50% (Fig. S2). On the contrary, pretreated with PtCD or PtCD@NP reversed the decrease of cell viability and restored it to more than 70%, suggesting that PtCD or PtCD@NP could protect cells from oxidative stress (Fig. S2). Exogenously ROSup was used to stimulate the overproduction of intracellular ROS and the O2 •- probe dihydroethidium (DHE) was utilized to detect the SOD-like activity of PtCD@NP. Compared with the control group, the red fluorescence was significantly enhanced in the ROSup group, while after adding PtCD or PtCD@NP in the ROSup group red fluorescence was significantly reduced, indicating a large number of O2 •- was produced by ROSup and the NPs effectively eliminated O2 •- by SOD-like activity (Fig. 4E).

The intercellular CAT-like activity of PtCD and PtCD@NP was studied by an O2-specifically quenched red fluorescence probe RDPP. The obvious red fluorescence signal was observed in the control group and the ROSup group, which was suppressed in the PtCD or PtCD@NP group, suggesting that PtCD or PtCD@NP had CAT-like activity that decomposed the H2O2 into O2 (Fig. 4G).

In addition, the DCFH-DA was used to detect the intracellular ROS level. Compared with the control group, a large number of DCF fluorescence signals were observed in the ROSup group, which decreased after treatment with PtCD and PtCD@NP (Fig. 4I). The flow cytometry results were consistent with the fluorescence images (Fig. 4F, H, and J).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The intracellular anti-inflammatory properties of PtCD@NP
ce:section.ce:para: Cytokines play a crucial role in the development of UC, and the imbalance of anti-inflammatory and pro-inflammatory cytokines leads to damage to intestinal barrier function and excessive immune responses.[30] RT-qPCR results showed that the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-12) increased after lipopolysaccharide (LPS) stimulation compared with the control group, while significantly decreased in PtCD or PtCD@NP-treated group (Fig. 4K). In addition, mRNA expression of the anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-10 was significantly up-regulated in the PtCD and PtCD@NP group compared with the LPS group (Fig. 4K). The results of RT-qPCR indicated that PtCD and PtCD@NP had anti-inflammatory effects by regulating cellular inflammatory factors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the targeting ability of PM@PtCD@NP
ce:section.ce:para: Platelets are closely associated with neutrophil swarming during inflammation.[31] Neutrophils attached to inflamed blood vessels extend P-selectin glycoprotein ligand-1 (PSGL-1) into the vessel lumen, where P-selectin scans the bloodstream for activated platelets.[32] Once activated, platelets bind to neutrophils and help neutrophils leak from the vascular lumen to the site of inflammation, further strengthening the recruitment of neutrophils to the site of inflammation and aggravating the inflammatory response.[33,34] To verify the targeting effect of platelet membrane on neutrophils, neutrophils were first extracted from the peripheral blood of mice, and their morphology and purity were observed by Wright-Giemsa staining. The images showed that the extracted neutrophils had rod-shaped or lobulated nuclei connected by slender narrow lobes, indicating that the extracted neutrophils were of high purity (Fig. S3).

Since neutrophils are suspended cells and their survival time in vitro is only about 8 h, phorbol myristate acetate (PMA) was used to stimulate neutrophils to become the inflammatory state in the cellular uptake experiment. After NPs were incubated with neutrophils for 2 h, compared with the membrane-free group (PtCD@NP), the membrane-coated group (PM@PtCD@NP) showed higher red fluorescent signals in cells, indicating more NPs were internalized ( Fig. 5A). Flow cytometry results were consistent with the fluorescent images, demonstrating that platelet membrane coating increased the targeting of NPs (Fig. 5B).

To evaluate the colon-targeting ability of PM@PtCD@NP, healthy mice and colitis mice were intravenously injected with DiR-labeled PM@PtCD@NP. The whole-body and the isolated organs' fluorescence images were taken by an in vivo imaging system (IVIS). Results showed that the fluorescence signal was strongest at 1 h, gradually weakened with time, and disappeared after 24 h both in vivo and in the isolated organs, indicating the gradual excretion of NPs (Fig. 5C-E). In addition, the fluorescence signal of colon tissues also reached the highest level at 1 h, indicating the effective uptake of PM@PtCD@NP by colons (Fig. 5C and F). The fluorescence intensity of healthy mice was weaker than that of colitis mice both in vivo and in organs, indicating that the platelet membrane-coated PM@PtCD@NP more effectively targeted the inflammatory sites of the colons (Fig. S4A-C). The half-life (t1/2) of PM@PtCD@NP in blood was measured as 2.03 ± 0.92 h (Fig. S4D). Then, the colons of colitis mice were made into frozen sections, and the localization of DiL-labeled NPs was observed in the colon, indicating that PM@PtCD@NP successfully reached the inflammation site of the colon after intravenous injection (Fig. S5).

SYK inhibitors, such as Pic, block the transmission of integrin signals in neutrophils, thereby inhibiting neutrophil adhesion and aggregation, promoting the “reverse migration” of neutrophils from inflammatory tissues into the bloodstream, and achieving the “braking” of neutrophils.[35] Therefore, PtCD was combined with Pic in the treatment of murine colitis to verify the therapeutic effect of PM@Pic/PtCD@NP. Before applying NPs to animal models, biosafety was first verified. MTT results showed that cell viability did not decrease significantly within 24 h or 48 h when the concentration of PM@Pic/PtCD@NP was lower than 200 μg/mL, and when the concentration was up to 400 μg/mL, the cell viability was still above 70%, indicating that PM@Pic/PtCD@NP had good biosafety and low cytotoxicity ( Fig. 6A and B).

The mice were injected intravenously with PM@Pic/PtCD@NP, and the weight change curve showed no significant weight loss in three doses of PM@Pic/PtCD@NP (5 mg/kg, 15 mg/kg, and 30 mg/kg) (Fig. 6C). And there was no hemolysis, proving that PM@Pic/PtCD@NP had good biocompatibility (Fig. 6D). Flow cytometry of the Annexin V-FITC/PI method demonstrated that the proportion of living cells was over 80% in the PM@Pic/PtCD@NP group, which further verified the good biosafety of the NPs (Fig. 6E). On the last day of the experiment, the mice were sacrificed, and the H&E staining results showed that the colon had no histological changes, the myocardial fibers were intact, hepatocytes and hepatic lobules were visible in the liver tissue, the margins of the spleen were clear, and the lungs and kidneys were also not diseased, showing no significant difference from the healthy control group (Fig. 6F). The blood routine and biochemical indexes indicated that white blood cells (WBC), red blood cells (RBC), neutrophil granulocytes, platelets, lymphocytes, and hemoglobin were all within the normal range (Fig. 6G). Glutamic-pyruvic transaminase (ALT), aspartate transaminase (AST), urea nitrogen (BUN), creatinine (CRE) reflecting renal function, creatine kinase (CK), and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) were all within the normal range, with no statistical difference compared with the healthy control group (Fig. 6H).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the therapeutic effect of PM@Pic/PtCD@NP in murine colitis
ce:section.ce:para: Mice were administered 2.5% DSS for 5 consecutive days to induce colitis ( Fig. 7A). The weight change curve in the control group remained stable, while the DSS-treated group continued to decrease (Fig. 7B). After treatment with PM@Pic@NP, PM@PtCD@NP, or PM@Pic/PtCD@NP, the degree of weight loss was reduced (Fig. 7B). The disease activity index (DAI) index includes weight change, fecal status, and fecal occult blood status. The change curve of the DAI index showed that there was no change in the control group (Fig. 7C). On the contrary, DAI in the DSS group continued to increase within 5 days (Fig. 7C). After intravenous administration of PM@Pic@NP, PM@PtCD@NP, or PM@Pic/PtCD@NP, the DAI index showed a decreasing trend, and PM@Pic/PtCD@NP-treated group decreased the most (Fig. 7C). Colon images and endoscopic images also demonstrated that the colons in the DSS group were shortened, and the colonic mucosa was bleeding, accompanied by large areas of redness, swelling, bleeding, and ulcers (Fig. 7D-F). However, after treatment with PM@Pic@NP, PM@PtCD@NP, or PM@Pic/PtCD@NP, the degree of colon shortening and mucosal congestion decreased, and there were almost no ulcers and hemorrhagic spots in the PM@Pic/PtCD@NP group, which was no different from that of the healthy control group (Fig. 7D-F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: PM@Pic/PtCD@NP are capable of downregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines
ce:section.ce:para: Dysregulation of mucosal immune response in colitis is often accompanied by the release of high levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines [36]. RT-qPCR results showed that compared with the control group, the mRNA levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-12, IL-6, and IL-1β) in the DSS group were significantly higher, and anti-inflammatory cytokines (IL-10) were down-regulated (Fig. 7G). However, after treating with PM@Pic@NP, PM@PtCD@NP, or PM@Pic/PtCD@NP, the levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines were decreased, while IL-10 was up-regulated (Fig. 7G). These results indicated that NPs alleviated inflammatory symptoms by suppressing the expression of pro-inflammatory factors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PM@Pic/PtCD@NP contributes to the restoration of the intestinal barrier
ce:section.ce:para: Uncontrolled activation of inflammatory factors leads to mucosal epithelial damage, so the immunofluorescence technique was used to verify the expression of tight junction protein (occludin, ZO-1, and claudin-1) in the colons. The fluorescence signals in the DSS group were significantly weaker than that of the control group, demonstrating that there is a loss of mucosal epithelial tight junction protein in DSS-induced colitis (Fig. 7H). However, compared with the DSS group, PM@Pic@NP, PM@PtCD@NP, or PM@Pic/PtCD@NP treatment restored the fluorescence signal to a certain extent, which suggested that NPs restored the function of tight junction protein (Fig. 7H).

To determine that Pic could “brake” neutrophils during inflammation, the proportion of CD11b+Ly6G+ neutrophils in leukocytes of lamina propria was quantitatively analyzed by flow cytometry. The results showed that the proportion of neutrophils in the control group was only 4.91%, while in the DSS group increased to 60.8%, indicating that neutrophils were recruited in the inflammatory tissue during UC ( Fig. 8A and S6). After treatment with PM@Pic@NP, PM@PtCD@NP, or PM@Pic/PtCD@NP, the neutrophils proportion was decreased, in which the PM@Pic/PtCD@NP group decreased the most and almost recovered to the level of the healthy control group (Fig. 8A and S6). H&E sections showed that the colons of mice in the control group contained a large number of goblet cells with almost no inflammatory cell infiltration and clear crypts, while colitis mice had no crypts and a large number of neutrophils (Fig. 8B). After treatment with PM@Pic@NP, PM@PtCD@NP, or PM@Pic/PtCD@NP, colon integrity was improved, the number of inflammatory cells was reduced, and crypts were visible (Fig. 8B). MPO is a peroxidase secreted primarily by neutrophils, and the activity of MPO in colon tissue indirectly reflects the swarming of neutrophils. Compared with the control group, the MPO activity in the DSS group was increased, while that in PM@Pic@NP, PM@PtCD@NP, or PM@Pic/PtCD@NP treated groups was significantly decreased, indicating that neutrophil infiltration was reduced after NPs treatment (Fig. S7). The immunofluorescence images showed that the fluorescence signal of MPO was almost not observed in the colon of the control group, while was significantly enhanced in the DSS group, indicating that a large number of neutrophils were recruited in the colon and secreted excessive MPO in UC (Fig. 8C). Compared with the DSS group, the fluorescence signal decreased after treatment with PM@Pic@NP, PM@PtCD@NP, or PM@Pic/PtCD@NP, indicating that Pic inhibited neutrophil swarming (Fig. 8C). These results indicated that DSS successfully induced colon inflammation in mice, such as weight loss, shortened colon length, increased DAI index, and neutrophil recruitment. The inflammatory symptoms were effectively relieved after intravenous administration of PM@Pic@NP, PM@PtCD@NP, or PM@Pic/PtCD@NP. Especially in the PM@Pic/PtCD@NP group, ROS-scavenging combined with “neutrophil braking” significantly reduced the level of inflammatory factors and neutrophil swarming, which had a good therapeutic effect on murine colitis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RNA-seq analysis of PM@Pic/PtCD@NP treatment in murine colitis
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the therapeutic mechanism of PM@Pic/PtCD@NP in murine colitis, colon tissues were obtained for RNA-Seq analysis. Firstly, the correlation analysis of the gene expression of the samples was carried out to evaluate the clustering among the samples. The results of principal component analysis (PCA) showed that the samples of each group had good repeatability and correlation, which could be used for the next RNA-seq analysis (Fig. S8). The intersection part of the Venn diagram contained 570 genes, that is, genes up-regulated in DSS-induced colitis mice and down-regulated after treatment with PM@Pic/PtCD@NP ( Fig. 9A). All the different genes were described using volcanic maps, in which 381 genes were up-regulated in red and 1087 genes were down-regulated in blue (Fig. 9B). The gene expression profiles of the DSS group and the control group were significantly different in the clustering heat map, while after PM@Pic/PtCD@NP treatment, the gene expression profiles became comparable to the control group (Fig. 9C). These results indicated that PM@Pic/PtCD@NP treatment effectively reversed the abnormal changes in gene expression profile induced by DSS.

The result of the gene ontology (GO) enrichment bubble map of differentially expressed genes indicated that the up-regulated genes were mostly related to “cell membrane” and “protein binding” (Fig. 9D). The Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) is a public database on signaling pathways in genome decoding. KEGG-level histogram analysis showed that the development of DSS-induced colitis in mice was correlated with tumor-related pathways (Fig. 9E). The gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) results showed that gene markers of mitochondrial respiratory chain complex Ⅰ were significantly associated with PM@Pic/PtCD@NP treatment (Fig. 9F). In addition, the GSEA of the KEGG pathway also showed that genes highly expressed in the PM@Pic/PtCD@NP-treated group were enriched in the set of genes associated with the intestinal immune network, which is used to produce immune globulin A (IgA) (Fig. 9G).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the preventive effect of PM@Pic/PtCD@NP in murine colitis
ce:section.ce:para: A murine colitis model was further established to verify the preventive effect of PM@Pic/PtCD@NP. C57BL/6 mice were given 2.5% DSS to induce colitis for 7 consecutive days, and NPs were injected intravenously every other day ( Fig. 10A). Results showed that compared with the healthy control group, the body weight of the DSS group decreased significantly, while the PM@Pic@NP, PM@PtCD@NP, and PM@Pic/PtCD@NP-treated group reversed the weight loss (Fig. 10B). Meanwhile, the DAI of the control group was stable at the lowest level within 7 days, while that of the DSS group showed a rapidly rising trend, and the DAI index decreased after treatment with PM@Pic@NP, PM@PtCD@NP, and PM@Pic/PtCD@NP (Fig. 10C). The colons were significantly shortened during colitis, which was reversed after treatment with PM@Pic@NP, PM@PtCD@NP, and PM@Pic/PtCD@NP, and there was no statistical difference between the PM@Pic/PtCD@NP group and the control group (Fig. 10D and S9A). Moreover, MPO activity also increased in the DSS group and returned to normal levels after NP treatment (Fig. 10E). Immunofluorescence staining of MPO also showed the same results (Fig. S9B). Fluorescence in the control group was weak, which was enhanced in the DSS group, indicating neutrophils swarming and increased MPO secretion during colitis. However, in the PM@Pic/PtCD@NP-treated group, the fluorescence signal was weakened and inflammation resolved (Fig. S9B).

In addition, the images of endoscopic exploration indicated that the colonic mucosa of the control group was complete and elastic, and the capillaries were clear, while a large number of ulcers, redness, and swelling were observed in colitis mice, accompanied by bleeding (Fig. 10F). Compared with the DSS group, the colonic mucosa status of PM@Pic@NP, PM@PtCD@NP, and PM@Pic/PtCD@NP group was significantly restored, especially in the PM@Pic/PtCD@NP group, the redness and swelling area were significantly reduced, and there was no ulceration area or bleeding points (Fig. 10F). The swarming of neutrophils was also observed by H&E staining of colon tissues. Almost no neutrophils were observed in the colon of the control group, and the structure was intact, while the laminae propria of the colon of the DSS group contained a large number of neutrophils, with crypt defects and reduced goblet cells (Fig. 10G and S9C). PM@Pic@NP, PM@PtCD@NP, and PM@Pic/PtCD@NP treatment reduced the number of neutrophils, effectively preventing neutrophil swarming (Fig. 10G and S9C).

Besides, compared with the control group, the fluorescence signals of occludin, ZO-1, and claudin-1 in the DSS group decreased significantly, while recovered in PM@Pic@NP, PM@PtCD@NP, and PM@Pic/PtCD@NP groups, indicating that PM@Pic@NP, PM@PtCD@NP, and PM@Pic/PtCD@NP restored the intestinal barrier (Fig. 10H). The expression of inflammatory factors in colon tissues was analyzed by RT-qPCR. The results showed that compared with the control group, pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-12, IL-6, and IL-1β) were increased in the DSS group, indicating that DSS successfully induced colitis (Fig. 10I). After treatment with PM@Pic@NP, PM@PtCD@NP, and PM@Pic/PtCD@NP, the expression level of proinflammatory cytokines was significantly decreased, and the expression level of anti-inflammatory factor IL-10 was increased (Fig. 10I).

In this work, PLGA-based polymer was used to encapsulate cascade nanozymes with Pic for drug delivery in the treatment of murine colitis. As a Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved polymer, PLGA has the characteristics of biodegradability, good biocompatibility, slow release and improved water solubility of drugs, which efficiently delivers drugs to the inflammation site of the colon and continuously release encapsulated drugs, making them ideal drug delivery carriers for UC treatment [37–39]. In addition, PLGA can encapsulate both hydrophilic and hydrophobic drugs through microemulsion technology [40]. Pic is a hydrophobic natural small-molecule compound, its solubility and bioavailability in water are improved after PLGA encapsulation.

Recently, our lab synthesized PtCD nanozymes successfully and identified their cascade SOD/CAT enzymatic activities. However, a small size of PtCD (about 3 nm) led to an easy clearance, a short body half-life of 53 min, and reduced efficacy of the drug [19]. PLGA-based polymer encapsulation effectively solved the above problems, increased the retention of drugs in the inflammatory site, and increased the half-life to 2.03 ± 0.92 h.

The SOD/CAT enzymatic activities of PtCD and PtCD@NP were evaluated, and it was found that the enzymatic activity decreased after PLGA encapsulation, and the dissolved oxygen content of PtCD was higher than that of PtCD@NP at the same time, suggesting the slow-release of PtCD from the core of PLGA. On the contrary, in the intracellular anti-inflammatory and anti-oxidant experiments, PtCD@NP had a better effect than PtCD, indicating that PLGA encapsulation improved the bioavailability and biosafety of PtCD.

Lysosomes are the primary organelles for phagocytose exogenous substances, while mitochondria are the main organelles generating O2 •- and inducing oxidative damage to cells, [41] so it would be critical for PtCD@NP to co-locate with mitochondria to scavenge ROS after being internalized by the cells. The PPC between PtCD@NP and lysosomes was 0.74, while that with mitochondria is 0.91, indicating that PtCD@NP and mitochondria are well co-located, and ROS were scavenged from the mitochondria.

Neutrophils are important immune cells in the gut. When bacteria invade, neutrophils migrate to the injured area and release inflammatory mediators to recruit more neutrophils and other defense cells. Persistent activation and over-recruitment of neutrophils are characteristic markers of UC [42]. Therefore, regulating the function of neutrophils and controlling the continuous activation and excessive swarming of neutrophils is a new way to prevent and treat UC. At present, a large number of studies have found that inflammation was alleviated by preventing neutrophils from migrating to the site of inflammation. Zhang et al. proposed the loaded Pic could be delivered to adherent neutrophils and detached into circulation, thus decreasing neutrophil infiltration and reducing infarct size in acute ischemic stroke [25]. Lu et al. stated that resveratrol reduced pro-inflammatory factors and neutrophil infiltration to alleviate IBD [43].

It is well documented that platelets act as accomplices in neutrophil infiltration at inflammation sites [44–46]. The unique surface of platelets can actively recognize adherent neutrophils through cell-cell recognition, provide accurate localization, and achieve drug enrichment of inflammatory surroundings [47]. The platelet-inspired nanomedicine platform containing PtCD SOD/CAT-like nanozymes and Pic was synthesized, after the PM@Pic/PtCD@NP was recognized by neutrophils, Pic was released from the NPs, promoting the detachment of the adherent neutrophils from the vascular wall, and then inhibiting the swarming of neutrophils into the inflammatory site. Moreover, the difference in the cumulative drug release of PtCD@NP and PM@PtCD@NP in PBS solution in pH 7.4 condition within 48 h may be due to the membrane coating that slows down the drug release rate.

GO is an international classification system of gene function, which is divided into three parts: molecular function, cell component, and biological process [48]. The result of the GO enrichment bubble map indicated that the platelet membrane coated on the biomimetic NPs may play an important role in the treatment by simulating the interaction between platelets and neutrophils in the state of inflammation to target neutrophils. Likewise, the KEGG is a public database on signaling pathways in genome decoding, which suggests that if UC is not treated promptly and effectively, it might develop into colorectal cancer. Based on the principle of gene set enrichment analysis for GO entries, GSEA results are generally considered that the gene set of |NES (normalized enrichment score)|> 1, NOM. pval (nominal p-value) < 0.05, FDR.qval (q value) < 0.25 is meaningful, [49] which further confirmed that PM@Pic/PtCD@NP was localized to mitochondria after uptake by cells, and played an anti-oxidant role by scavenging ROS from ROS-producing organelles. In addition, IgA is an important switch to control gut microbiota and is involved in intestinal immune regulation, [50] which inspires further exploration of the specific mechanism of regulating gut microbiota.

In summary, the therapeutic effects of PM@Pic/PtCD@NP on free radicals, neutrophil swarming, and murine colitis were evaluated. It was suggested that PM@Pic/PtCD@NP hitchhike to the site of inflammation, scavenge ROS, inhibit neutrophil swarming, reduce the expression of pro-inflammatory factors, restore the function of the intestinal epithelial barrier, and prevent and reverse murine colitis. However, further studies are needed to more precisely determine the dosage and administration frequency of PM@Pic/PtCD@NP for bulk preparation and translational applications.
